_patch_meta.description: Phase 6 anchor selection + Phase 7 verse routing. Routes all P-status and G-status verses to M46-A, M46-B, or M46-C. Sets is_anchor=1 for 4 anchor verses (2 per A and B, 1 for C primary + 1 secondary). G-status verses use VCREVISE to add cluster_subgroup_id without disturbing existing group_id. P-status and UT verses use VCVERSE to route to sub-group.
notes: G3045 liparos (mti_id=4702) has no sub-group assignment in DB (H6 warning). Add mti_term_subgroup row: M46-A.
sha.a.nan 413-002 verses need separate identification from §3 detail — routed to M46-C; CC to verify which vc_ids carry group_id=1862.
9 UT borderline verses (from Phase 2) remain unrouted pending researcher decision — not included in this patch.
Several duplicate vc_ids may exist where both the 'old' vc_id and a 'new' vc_id reference the same verse/term — CC to deduplicate before applying.
Pro 6:31 hon was revised to SA in Phase 2; Pro 19:14 hon vc_id=219934 is P — included in routing.

operations.rationale: liparos names luxurious goods for which the soul (psuchē) longed; material richness as the object of corrupted inner desire in Babylon's judgment context.
rov names the multitude of words — verbal excess as inner-being phenomenon; the talkative person whose words demand moral assessment.
rov names the greatness/magnitude of iniquity and hatred — the weight of inner moral distortion that occasions divine punishment.
da.shen (grown fat) names the condition of material satiation that produces inner turning from God — the prosperity-to-apostasy sequence.
da.shen (refreshes) names the inner-physical renewal produced by good news — the ordered reception of abundance (good news) refreshing the deep frame of the person.
o.sher: 'those who trust in their wealth and boast of the abundance of their riches' — riches as object of misplaced trust and boasting.
o.sher: the man 'trusted in the abundance of his riches and sought refuge in his own destruction' — riches replacing God as refuge.
o.sher: violent men get riches — the moral character (violence) that produces wealth; wealth revealing the inner person.
o.sher: 'whoever trusts in his riches will fall' — riches as the object of misplaced trust producing the fall.
o.sher: wisdom's left hand holds riches and honor — wealth as the gift of Wisdom; wealth ordered through character.
o.sher: 'riches and honor are with me, enduring wealth and righteousness' — Wisdom claiming riches and righteousness as unified; ordered wealth through Wisdom.
o.sher: 'wealth and riches are in his house, and his righteousness endures forever' — the righteous person's wealth paired with enduring righteousness.
o.sher: Solomon chose not to ask for riches — the king who subordinated wealth to wisdom; wealth as the test of inner priority.
o.sher: God gives riches as gift to one who chose wisdom — ordered wealth as divine gift following right inner priority.
o.sher: Solomon's heart did not ask for possessions or wealth but for wisdom — the inner priority that reveals character.
o.sher: riches given as gift after wisdom was chosen — wealth as divine gift following right inner orientation.
o.sher: 'both riches and honor come from you, and you rule over all' — riches as divine gift acknowledging God's sovereignty over material provision.
o.sher: 'another pretends to be poor, yet has great wealth' — the concealment of wealth as deceptive inner posture; wealth and self-presentation.
o.sher: a man's riches may ransom his life — wealth as moral-relational resource with life-determining weight.
o.sher: 'a good name is to be chosen rather than great riches' — wisdom placing inner character (name) above material wealth.
o.sher: 'the reward of humility and fear of the Lord is riches and honor and life' — the inner disposition (humility, fear) producing ordered wealth.
o.sher (second instance): enduring wealth with righteousness as Wisdom's gift.
o.sher: 'there is no end to all his toil, and his eyes are never satisfied with riches' — the insatiable labor for riches; constitutionally unsatisfied inner person.
o.sher: 'the abundance of the rich will not let him sleep' — wealth disturbing the inner rest; the restlessness of abundance.
o.sher: riches 'kept by their owner to his hurt' — hoarded wealth becoming its owner's damage.
o.sher: 'those riches were lost in a bad venture' — the precariousness of material wealth and the grief of loss.
o.sher: 'everyone also to whom God has given wealth and possessions and power to enjoy them... this is the gift of God' — wealth as divinely given gift when received with the capacity to enjoy it.
o.sher: God gives wealth but not the power to enjoy it — a stranger enjoys it; the alienation between having wealth and being able to enjoy it.
o.sher: Haman's self-display of riches, sons, and honor before his friends — wealth as the ground of pride and self-glorification.
plousios: 'with difficulty will a rich person enter the kingdom' — wealth as the obstacle to kingdom entry; the spiritual weight of material richness.
plousios: camel through eye of needle — graphic statement of the kingdom-blocking character of wealth.
plousios: same camel-needle saying — wealth as kingdom obstacle.
plousios: 'woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation' — wealth as eschatological consolation that exhausts itself in this age.
plousios: the rich man whose land produced plentifully — parable opening establishing the moral context.
plousios: rich man with a dishonest manager — moral category context.
plousios: the rich man feasting sumptuously, ignoring Lazarus — wealth producing moral blindness to the suffering neighbor.
plousios: Lazarus desired to be fed from rich man's table — the indifference of wealth to the poor at the gate.
plousios: the rich man died — the eschatological reversal of material wealth.
plousios: 'he became very sad, for he was extremely rich' — wealth producing grief when confronted with kingdom demands.
plousios: camel-needle — wealth as kingdom obstacle.
plousios: the rich putting gifts in the treasury — the giving practice of the wealthy as moral observation (context for the widow's greater gift).
plousios: 'charge them not to be haughty, nor to set their hopes on the uncertainty of riches' — the twin distortions of wealth: haughtiness and misplaced hope.
plousios: 'the rich in his humiliation' — the rich person's glory fades like grass; wealth as that which passes away.
plousios: 'the rich man fade away in the midst of his pursuits' — the transience of wealth and the rich person absorbed in its pursuit.
plousios: God chose the poor to be 'rich in faith' — poverty of material condition paired with richness of faith; kingdom inversion of wealth categories.
plousios: 'are not the rich the ones who oppress you and drag you into court?' — wealth as the basis of oppressive power over the poor.
plousios: 'come now, you rich, weep and howl for the miseries coming upon you' — prophetic-eschatological judgment on wealth accumulated through injustice.
plousios: Smyrna — 'I know your poverty, but you are rich' — divine reversal: material poverty concealing actual inner richness before God.

ANCHOR M46-A. plousios: 'I am rich, I have prospered, I need nothing' — the self-declaration of wealth as the definitive inner-being distortion; false self-assessment concealing actual wretchedness. The cluster's most precise statement.
plousios: the rich among those hiding from the wrath of the Lamb — wealth providing no refuge in the day of judgment.
plousios: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' — Christ's pre-incarnate wealth voluntarily surrendered; the Christological paradigm.
plousios: 'God being rich in mercy' — wealth vocabulary applied to the divine attribute of mercy; God's mercy is inexhaustible/abundant.
plousios: rich and poor alike marked by the beast — wealth providing no exemption from the mark; material status irrelevant before eschatological allegiance demands.
plouteō: 'the rich he has sent away empty' — the Magnificat's reversal of the materially rich; wealth as the condition that leaves one empty before God.
plouteō: 'lays up treasure for himself and is not rich toward God' — the self-directed form of being rich vs being rich toward God; wealth-orientation as inner decision.
plouteō: 'bestowing his riches on all who call on him' — God's generosity toward all who call; wealth vocabulary for divine inexhaustible provision.
plouteō: 'already you have become rich! without us you have become kings!' — ironic address to Corinthians' self-satisfied spiritual wealth; false self-assessment of inner richness.
plouteō: 'so that you by his poverty might become rich' — the Christological wealth-exchange; poverty as the means of others' enrichment.
plouteō: 'those who desire to be rich fall into temptation and a snare' — the inner desire to become rich as the source of ruin.
plouteō: 'rich in good works' — wealth vocabulary applied to generous works; being rich toward others as the ordered form of wealth.
plouteō: 'I have prospered' — the self-declaration of prospering as false inner assessment (same verse as anchor A1).
plouteō: 'buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may be rich' — the divine counsel to seek true wealth through testing; the ordered alternative to false self-assessed wealth.
plouteō: merchants 'have grown rich from the power of her luxurious living' — Babylon's wealth-generating corruption; wealth from exploitative luxury.
plouteō: merchants who 'gained wealth from her' standing far off at her judgment — wealth from Babylon weeping at its loss.
plouteō: 'all who had ships at sea grew rich by her wealth' — Babylon's wealth enriching through commercial exploitation; lament at its loss.
ploutos: 'deceitfulness of riches choke the word' — riches as a choking force; the deceptive quality of wealth that prevents fruitfulness.
ploutos: 'cares of the world and deceitfulness of riches choke the word' — same pattern; riches choking inner responsiveness.
ploutos: 'choked by cares and riches and pleasures of life' — riches as one of three choking forces preventing fruit.
ploutos: 'extreme poverty overflowed in a wealth of generosity' — poverty producing generosity; material poverty generating relational/moral wealth.
ploutos: 'nor to set their hopes on the uncertainty of riches' — riches as uncertain hope-object vs God as reliable provider.
ploutos: Moses 'considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt' — eschatological revaluation placing reproach above material treasure; the ordered inner assessment.
ploutos: 'your riches have rotted' — the decay of accumulated wealth as judgment on unjust hoarding.
ploutizō: 'as poor, yet making many rich' — the apostolic paradox of material poverty generating spiritual-relational enrichment in others; Christological pattern in ministry.
ploutizō: 'enriched in every way to be generous in every way' — enrichment received for the purpose of generosity; wealth as through-put for others' benefit.

ANCHOR M46-C. ptōcheuō: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' — the Christological wealth-exchange; voluntary impoverishment as the paradigm of the ordered response to the wealth domain.

ptōchos: 'poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven' — poverty of spirit as the inner posture of dependence and humility before God; material poverty vocabulary transferred to name inner disposition.
ptōchos: 'the poor have good news preached to them' — the poor as the primary recipients of the gospel; poverty as the condition of openness to divine gift.
ptōchos: 'sell what you possess and give to the poor' — the command to voluntary impoverishment for others' benefit as the path to perfection.
ptōchos: 'blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God' — material poverty as eschatological blessing; the poor as heirs of the kingdom.
ptōchos: 'the poor have good news preached to them' — messianic proclamation to the poor as sign of the kingdom's arrival.
ptōchos: 'anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor' — Jesus's inaugural programmatic declaration; the poor as the primary address of the messianic mission.
ptōchos: 'invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind' — the commanded reorientation of hospitality from the wealthy to the poor.
ptōchos: 'bring in the poor and crippled' — the commanded inclusion of the poor in the banquet as kingdom pattern.
ptōchos: 'distribute to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven' — voluntary impoverishment for the poor producing eschatological wealth.
ptōchos: Zacchaeus giving half to the poor as restitution — the ordered reorientation of wealth toward the poor as sign of inner transformation.
ptōchos: 'this poor widow has put in more than all' — the greater generosity of the poor; poverty enabling genuine giving.
ptōchos: poor widow putting in two copper coins — the generosity of material poverty.
ptōchos: 'this poor widow has put in more than all' — the widow's gift declared greater; poverty enabling full giving.
ptōchos: 'sell all that you have and give to the poor' — the command to voluntary impoverishment for the sake of the poor and kingdom.
ptōchos: 'as poor, yet making many rich' — the apostolic poverty that enriches others; material poverty as the ground of spiritual-relational generosity.
ptōchos: the poor man in shabby clothes entering the assembly — the assembly's treatment of the poor as moral test.
ptōchos: 'say to the poor man, stand over there' — wealth-based preferential treatment as moral failure.
ptōchos: 'God chose those who are poor... to be rich in faith' — the poor chosen for kingdom richness; divine inversion of wealth categories.
ptōchos: 'the rich oppress you and drag you into court' — the structural violence of wealth against the poor.
ptōchos: Judas cared nothing for the poor but used the poor as pretext for theft — false concern for the poor masking inner greed.
ptōchos: 'the poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham's side' — the eschatological reversal; the poor man's destination contrasting with the rich man's.
ptōchos: 'you are... poor' — the Laodicean church's actual poverty despite self-assessed wealth.
a.shir: the rich man unwilling to take from his own flock — wealth and unwillingness to give; the moral character revealed by how the rich treat what they have.
a.shir: 'he goes to bed rich, but will do so no more; he opens his eyes and his wealth is gone' — the sudden loss of the wicked rich man's wealth.
a.shir: 'your rich men are full of violence' — wealth directly connected to inner violence and moral corruption.
a.shir: 'a rich man's wealth is his strong city' — wealth as the rich person's perceived security and stronghold; misplaced trust.
a.shir: 'the rich has many friends' — the relational distortion wealth creates; false friendship attached to wealth.
a.shir: 'a rich man's wealth is his strong city, and like a high wall in his imagination' — wealth as imagined fortress; the illusion of security.
a.shir: 'the rich answers roughly' — wealth producing harsh, dismissive inner orientation toward others.
a.shir: 'the rich and the poor meet together; the Lord is the maker of them both' — shared creatureliness before God; wealth does not create a different moral category before the Creator.
a.shir: 'the rich rules over the poor, and the borrower is the slave of the lender' — wealth as structural power producing relational domination.
a.shir: 'better is a poor man who walks in his integrity than a rich man who is crooked in his ways' — integrity as superior to wealth; character the true measure.
a.shir: 'a rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor man who has understanding will find him out' — wealth producing false self-assessed wisdom.
a.shir: 'the abundance of the rich will not let him sleep' — wealth producing inner restlessness; the cost of abundance in sleep and peace.
a.shir: 'the rich sit in a low place' — social inversion as wisdom observation about wealth and honor.
a.shar: Abram refusing to take anything lest the king say 'I made Abram rich' — the deliberate refusal of wealth to preserve integrity; not-enriching oneself through alliance.
a.shar: 'the Lord makes poor and makes rich; he brings low and he exalts' — divine sovereignty over material condition; wealth and poverty as instruments of God's governance.
a.shar: 'he will not be rich, and his wealth will not endure' — the wicked man's wealth failing; the futility of unjust accumulation.
a.shar: 'the blessing of the Lord makes rich, and he adds no sorrow with it' — divine blessing as the proper source of wealth; wealth from blessing carries no sorrow unlike wealth from toil.
a.shar: 'the hand of the diligent makes rich' — diligence as the inner character that produces wealth; character determining material outcome.
a.shar: 'one pretends to be rich, yet has nothing' — false self-presentation of wealth; deception in the wealth domain.

a.shar: 'whoever loves pleasure will be a poor man; he who loves wine and oil will not be rich' — the inner orientation toward pleasure as the path to poverty; love of luxury as the path away from wealth.

a.shar: 'do not toil to acquire wealth; be discerning enough to desist' — the wisdom-shaped restraint on the wealth-seeking drive; discernment as the inner discipline that stops the acquisition momentum.
a.shar: 'whoever hastens to be rich will not go unpunished' — the inner drive toward quick wealth as moral failure producing punishment.
a.shar: 'be not afraid when a man becomes rich' — the instruction not to fear the prosperity of the wicked; wealth not the true measure of standing before God.
a.shar: 'I am rich; I have found wealth for myself' — Ephraim's self-declaration of wealth as false moral vindication; becoming rich as the basis of self-assessed righteousness.
a.shar: 'blessed be the Lord, I have become rich' — corrupt shepherds self-congratulating on their enrichment while exploiting the flock; wealth as the marker of successful exploitation.
hon: 'sold your people for a trifle' — hon as the measure of worth; being sold for no-worth names the depth of covenantal abandonment.
hon: the wicked enticing 'we shall find all precious goods' — hon as the object of sinful inner desire; material goods as the goal of violence.
hon: 'enduring wealth and righteousness' — hon as wisdom's gift alongside righteousness; ordered wealth through Wisdom.
hon: 'a rich man's wealth is his strong city' — wealth as perceived psychological fortress; misplaced security.
hon: 'the diligent man will get precious wealth' — hon as fruit of diligence; character producing wealth.
hon: 'one pretends to be rich, yet has nothing' — deceptive self-presentation regarding wealth.
hon: 'wealth gained hastily will dwindle; whoever gathers little by little will increase' — character orientation (patience vs haste) determining wealth's sustainability.
hon: 'wealth brings many new friends, but a poor man is deserted' — wealth distorting authentic relationship; false friendship attached to material status.
hon: 'house and wealth are inherited from fathers, but a prudent wife is from the Lord' — inherited material wealth contrasted with divine gift of wisdom.
hon: 'by knowledge the rooms are filled with precious riches' — inner knowledge as the source that fills with material abundance.
hon: 'multiplies wealth by interest and profit gathers it for him who is generous to the poor' — unjust accumulation providentially redirected; moral corruption of unjust gain.
hon: 'four never say Enough' — constitutional insatiability; the inner person who cannot be satisfied.
sha.men: 'by them he lives in luxury, and his food is rich' — Babylon's military success producing luxury and rich food; material richness from conquest as inner condition of the conqueror.
sha.men: 'bread, the produce of the ground, which will be rich and plenteous' — eschatological divine provision producing rich bread; ordered abundance as divine gift.
sha.men: 'a rich land' — abundance of the land given to Israel which then ate, grew fat, and turned from God; the context for the prosperity-apostasy sequence.
sha.men: 'the large and rich land that you set before them, they did not serve you' — the rich land as the context of Israel's covenantal failure; abundance producing disobedience.
mish.man: 'wasting sickness among his stout warriors' — the fat/robust warriors of Assyria struck with wasting; human prosperity/robustness under divine judgment.
mish.man: 'the fat of his flesh will grow lean' — Jacob's material prosperity/fatness stripped in judgment; the reduction of material abundance as divine action.
sha.men: 'they have grown fat and sleek; they know no bounds in deeds of evil' — fatness producing moral insensibility and abandonment of justice.
da.shen: 'grown fat, they will turn to other gods and despise me' — material prosperity producing inner turning from God; the prosperity-apostasy pattern.
ANCHOR M46-A. sha.men: 'make the heart of this people dull' — God causing the heart to grow fat and lose perception; the judicial making-fat of the heart as the paradigm of distortion.
ANCHOR M46-B. da.shen: 'the soul of the diligent is richly supplied' — inner richness (ne.phesh + da.shen) as the fruit of character; the defining statement that the wealth domain touches the soul.
cho.sen: 'in the house of the righteous there is much treasure, but trouble befalls the income of the wicked' — cho.sen as the wealth of the righteous; character determining the quality of wealth.
ANCHOR M46-C. autarkēs: 'I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content' — contentment as a learned inner disposition; the paradigmatic statement of the ordered response.
sha.a.nan (413-001): 'woe to those who are at ease in Zion' — complacent ease produced by material security as vice.
sha.a.nan (413-001): 'exceedingly angry with the nations that are at ease' — divine anger at the complacent ease of the nations.
sha.a.nan (413-001): 'in the thought of one who is at ease there is contempt for misfortune' — at-ease person's contempt for the suffering of others.
sha.a.nan (413-001): 'the scorn of those who are at ease, of the contempt of the proud' — complacent ease producing contempt toward the oppressed.
sha.a.nan (413-001): 'your complacency has come into my ears' — Assyria's complacent ease as the inner ground of arrogance against God.
a.dan (3836-001): 'delighted themselves in your great goodness' — inner joy in God's abundant provision; delight in divine abundance as the ordered response.
cha.san (7109-001, negated): 'will not be stored or hoarded' — the eschatological reversal of hoarding; wealth released for the Lord's people.

da.shen (111-001): inner refreshment/enrichment of satisfied soul from generosity and trust — routes to M46-B (character producing wealth/inner richness).

sha.men verb (4697-002): spiritual fatness of prosperity turning heart from God — Deu 32:15 (Jeshurun grew fat and kicked) and Neh 9:25 (ate, grew fat, delighted but then turned). Routes to M46-A.
sha.men verb (4697-002): spiritual fatness of prosperity turning heart from God — Deu 32:15 (Jeshurun grew fat and kicked) and Neh 9:25 (ate, grew fat, delighted but then turned). Routes to M46-A.
